Chapter 5 • Diagnostic procedures
saphenous veins, with an elastic band tourniquet held with  are likely to cause haemolysis and are best reserved for 
a set of needle drivers or mosquito forceps. repeat sampling of small volumes, such as for serial blood 
The phlebotomy kit for guinea pigs should consist of  glucose measurements. Small volumes can be drawn 
equipment appropriate for their small size. Needle sizes  directly into insulin syringe barrels via a 23–25 G needle 
used range from 22 G to 29 G. Small-gauge needles such  with or without the plunger, with an EDTA or lithium-heparin 
as the 27 G and 29 G needles supplied with insulin syringes  microhaematocrit tube held below the needle hub or a 
heparinized capillary tube held inside the needle hub 
(Figure 5.3). A smear should be made immediately from 
fresh blood from the needle hub.
Larger volumes of 0.5–2 ml can be collected from the 
jugular vein or the cranial vena cava using a 22–25 G needle 
and a 2–3 ml syringe. Heparinizing the needle and syringe 
by drawing up heparin and then ejecting it before collect-
ing the blood sample helps to prevent clotting when the 
blood flow is slow due to sedation or dehydration, or when 
collecting from smaller peripheral veins. Sample quality is 
improved by the immediate and gentle delivery of the blood 
into collection vessels without needles attached. Applying 
pressure to the collection site for at least 30 seconds after 
venepuncture prevents haematoma formation.

Adhesive tape sampling
Indications
Adhesive tape sampling is indicated to investigate alope-
cia, pruritus, scaling, seborrhoea or altered hair colour, 
coat density or texture.
Method
1. Shave the hair if necessary to allow the 
tape to contact the skin lesion directly.
2. Gently squeeze or roll the skin between 
(a) gloved fingers to encourage exudate.
3. Apply the tape to the skin and peel it off 
to remove hairs, superficial skin cells and 
exudates.
4. Add one or two drops of stain on to a 
glass microscope slide and apply the 
tape over it.
Tests
Staining with thiazine purple dye (Diff-Quik™ solution 2) 
or blue ink facilitates the identification of fungal elements, 
fur mites and inflammatory cells.

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
Collection of samples from skin lesions for 
culture
Culture and susceptibility testing of samples from 
skin lesions is indicated for the identification of 
infectious organisms that have not been identified 
by in-clinic cytology, and for the detection of 
antimicrobial drug resistance to enable targeted 
treatment plans to be devised.
It is essential to avoid surface contamination for 
the results of skin culture to be valid. This can be 
achieved by passing a sterile swab under a freshly 
lifted scab, by fine-needle aspiration from inflamed 
lesions, or by incisional biopsy. Samples for fungal 
culture for ringworm (dermatophytosis) can be 
collected by combing the fur with a freshly 
unwrapped toothbrush.
Skin scrapes
Materials
This procedure requires hair clippers, a slightly blunted 
No. 10 or No. 15 scalpel blade dipped in clean mineral oil,  (a)
glass microscope slides and a microscope.
Method
1. Video 5.1 and Figure 5.8 show examples of skin 
scraping.
2. Shave the hair from the site to be sampled.
3. Apply a drop of liquid paraffin to the scalpel blade 
to help retain the material that is collected. This 
method also leaves any mites that are present alive 
and free to move within the oil.
4. First, make broad sweeps of the blade held at 90 
degrees to the plane of the skin to remove the hair 
(if not already shaved). This will reveal the fur-
clasping mites C. caviae. (These mites can also be 
identified in hair plucks, as described above.)
5. Then, take deep scrapings to examine for Sarcoptes 
spp. and Trixacarus caviae. These are made with 
the blade still held at 90 degrees to the plane of the 
skin, focusing on scraping a small area of skin, 
aiming to collect hair and several layers of skin. 
Between scrapes, gently squeeze the area to 
encourage capillary leakage and enable collection 
of material from deeper layers. (b)
6. Wipe the blade on to a glass slide.
5.8 (a, b) Technique for taking scrapings from a skin lesion.
7. Examine the entire slide under a low-power (X10) 
lens, then magnify areas of interest using a lens up 
to X40, looking for live mites (Figure 5.9).
8. Using a separate slide, make an impression smear of 
the scraped site (as described above). Examine the 
slide for mites, bacteria, yeast and inflammatory cells.
Microscopic appearance of a Trixacarus caviae mite

Tests
Histopathology, culture and susceptibility testing, and 
examination of impression smears should be undertaken.
Lymph node samples
Enlarged lymph nodes should be sampled.
■  Enlarged peripheral lymph nodes can be 
readily identified on physical examination.  
The most prominent and easily palpable lymph 
nodes when enlarged tend to be the subman-
dibular, inguinal and popliteal nodes.
■  Enlarged abdominal lymph nodes can some-
times be palpated as freely moving abdominal 
masses; in other cases they may not be 
identified unless imaging is undertaken.
■  Enlarged mediastinal and retropharyngeal 
lymph nodes are usually identified only on 
(a)
imaging.
Fine-needle aspiration
Fine-needle aspiration of lymph nodes produces valuable 
samples for cytology and culture. Cytology is the most 
useful for differentiating between neoplasia and absces-
sation of lymph nodes but may not allow differentiation 
between neoplasia and generalized severe inflammation 
or lymphadenopathy and may be of limited value if the 
node is too dense and poorly exfoliative.
